title: Filter (mathematics)
content:
In mathematics, a filter or order filter is a special subset of a partially ordered set. Filters appear in order and lattice theory, but can also be found in topology, from where they originate. The dual notion of a filter is an order ideal. Filters were introduced by Henri Cartan in 1937 and subsequently used by Bourbaki in their book "Topologie Générale" as an alternative to the similar notion of a net developed in 1922 by E. H. Moore and H. L. Smith. Motivation.

Intuitively, a filter in a partially ordered set (), formula_1 is a subset of formula_2 that includes as members those elements that are large enough to satisfy some given criterion. For example, if formula_3 is an element of the poset, then the set of elements that are above formula_3 is a filter, called the principal filter at formula_5 (If formula_3 and formula_7 are incomparable elements of the poset, then neither of the principal filters at formula_3 and formula_7 is contained in the other one, and conversely.) Similarly, a filter on a set contains those subsets that are sufficiently large to contain some given.

For example, if the set is the real line and formula_3 is one of its points, then the family of sets that include formula_3 in their interior is a filter, called the filter of neighbourhoods of formula_5 The in this case is slightly larger than formula_13 but it still does not contain any other specific point of the line. The above interpretations explain conditions 1 and 3 in the section General definition: Clearly the empty set is not "large enough", and clearly the collection of "large enough" things should be "upward-closed".

However, they do not really, without elaboration, explain condition 2 of the general definition. For, why should two "large enough" things contain a "large enough" thing? Alternatively, a filter can be viewed as a "locating scheme": When trying to locate something (a point or a subset) in the space formula_14 call a filter the collection of subsets of formula_15 that might contain "what is looked for".

Then this "filter" should possess the following natural structure: An ultrafilter can be viewed as a "perfect locating scheme" where subset formula_16 of the space formula_15 can be used in deciding whether "what is looked for" might lie in formula_21 From this interpretation, compactness (see the mathematical characterization below) can be viewed as the property that "no location scheme can end up with nothing", or, to put it another way, "always something will be found".

The mathematical notion of filter provides a precise language to treat these situations in a rigorous and general way, which is useful in analysis, general topology and logic. General definition: Filter on a partially ordered set. A subset formula_22 of a partially ordered set formula_23 is an order filter if the following conditions hold: where it is said to be proper if in addition formula_22 is not equal to the whole set formula_36 Depending on the author, the term filter is either a synonym of order filter or else it refers to a order filter. This article defines filter to mean order filter.

While the above definition is the most general way to define a filter for arbitrary posets, it was originally defined for lattice s only. In this case, the above definition can be characterized by the following equivalent statement: A subset formula_22 of a lattice formula_23 is a filter, if and only if it is a non-empty upper set that is closed under finite infima (or meet s), that is, for all formula_26 it is also the case that formula_40 A subset formula_41 of formula_22 is a filter basis if the upper set generated by formula_41 is all of formula_44 Note that every filter is its own basis.

The smallest filter that contains a given element formula_45 is a principal filter and formula_46 is a principal element in this situation. The principal filter for formula_46 is just given by the set formula_48 and is denoted by prefixing formula_46 with an upward arrow: The dual notion of a filter, that is, the concept obtained by reversing all formula_50 and exchanging formula_51 with formula_52 is ideal. Because of this duality, the discussion of filters usually boils down to the discussion of ideals. Hence, most additional information on this topic (including the definition of maximal filters and prime filters) is to be found in the article on ideals.

There is a separate article on ultrafilter s. Filter on a set. Definition of a filter. There are two competing definitions of a "filter on a set," both of which require that a filter be a. One definition defines "filter" as a synonym of "dual ideal" while the other defines "filter" to mean a dual ideal that is also. Given a set formula_53 a canonical partial ordering formula_54 can be defined on the powerset formula_55 by subset inclusion, turning formula_56 into a lattice. A "dual ideal" is just a filter with respect to this partial ordering.

Note that if formula_57 then there is exactly one dual ideal on formula_53 which is formula_59 Filter definition 1: Dual ideal. The article uses the following definition of "filter on a set." Filter definition 2: Proper dual ideal. The other definition of "filter on a set" is the original definition of a "filter" given by Henri Cartan, which required that a filter on a set be a dual ideal that does contain the empty set: The only non-proper filter on formula_41 is formula_61 Much mathematical literature, especially that related to Topology, defines "filter" to mean a dual ideal.

Filter bases, subbases, and comparison. A subset formula_62 of formula_55 is called a prefilter, filter base, or filter basis if formula_62 is non-empty and the intersection of any two members of formula_62 is a superset of some member(s) of formula_66 If the empty set is not a member of formula_67 we say formula_62 is a proper filter base.

Given a filter base formula_67 the filter generated or spanned by formula_62 is defined as the minimum filter containing formula_66 It is the family of all those subsets of formula_41 which are supersets of some member(s) of formula_66 Every filter is also a filter base, so the process of passing from filter base to filter may be viewed as a sort of completion.

For every subset formula_74 of formula_55 there is a smallest (possibly nonproper) filter formula_22 containing formula_77 called the filter generated or spanned by formula_78 Similarly as for a filter spanned by a, a filter spanned by a formula_74 is the minimum filter containing formula_78 It is constructed by taking all finite intersections of formula_77 which then form a filter base for formula_44 This filter is proper if and only if every finite intersection of elements of formula_74 is non-empty, and in that case we say that formula_74 is a filter subbase.

If formula_62 and formula_86 are two filter bases on formula_53 one says formula_86 is finer than formula_62 (or that formula_86 is a refinement of formula_62) if for each formula_92 there is a formula_93 such that formula_94 If also formula_62 is finer than formula_96 one says that they are equivalent filter bases. Filters in model theory. For every filter formula_22 on a set formula_41 the set function defined by formula_137 is finitely additive — a "measure" if that term is construed rather loosely. Therefore, the statement formula_138 can be considered somewhat analogous to the statement that formula_139 holds "almost everywhere".

That interpretation of membership in a filter is used (for motivation, although it is not needed for actual) in the theory of ultraproduct s in model theory, a branch of mathematical logic. Filters in topology. In topology and analysis, filters are used to define convergence in a manner similar to the role of sequence s in a metric space. In topology and related areas of mathematics, a filter is a generalization of a net.

Both nets and filters provide very general contexts to unify the various notions of limit to arbitrary topological space s. A sequence is usually indexed by the natural numbers formula_140 which are a totally ordered set. Thus, limits in first-countable space s can be described by sequences. However, if the space is not first-countable, nets or filters must be used. Nets generalize the notion of a sequence by requiring the index set simply be a directed set. Filters can be thought of as sets built from multiple nets.

Therefore, both the limit of a filter and the limit of a net are conceptually the same as the limit of a sequence. Throughout, formula_15 will be a topological space and formula_142 Neighbourhood bases. Take formula_143 to be the neighbourhood filter at point formula_3 for formula_145 This means that formula_143 is the set of all topological neighbourhood s of the point formula_5 It can be verified that formula_143 is a filter. A neighbourhood system is another name for a neighbourhood filter.

To say that formula_149 is a neighbourhood base at formula_3 for formula_15 means that each subset formula_41 of formula_15 is a neighbourhood of formula_3 if and only if there exists formula_155 Every neighbourhood base at formula_3 is a filter base that generates the neighbourhood filter at formula_5 Convergent filter bases. To say that a filter base formula_62 converge s to formula_13 denoted formula_160 means that for every neighbourhood formula_161 of formula_13 there is a formula_163 such that formula_164 In this case, formula_3 is called a limit of formula_62 and formula_62 is called a convergent filter base.

Every neighbourhood base formula_168 of formula_3 converges to formula_5 For formula_179 the following are equivalent: Indeed: (i) implies (ii): if formula_22 is a filter base satisfying the properties of (i), then the filter associated to formula_22 satisfies the properties of (ii). (ii) implies (iii): if formula_161 is any open neighborhood of formula_3 then by the definition of convergence, formula_161 contains an element of formula_22; since also formula_206 formula_161 and formula_181 have non-empty intersection. (iii) implies (i): Define formula_209 Then formula_22 is a filter base satisfying the properties of (i). Clustering.

A filter base formula_62 on formula_15 is said to cluster at formula_3 (or have formula_3 as a cluster point) if and only if each element of formula_62 has non-empty intersection with each neighbourhood of formula_5 For a filter base formula_67 the set formula_227 is the set of all cluster points of formula_62 (the closure of formula_229 is formula_230 Assume that formula_15 is a complete lattice. Properties of a topological space. If formula_15 is a topological space then: Functions between topological spaces. Let formula_15 and formula_181 be topological spaces, let formula_108 be a filter base on formula_14 and let formula_250 be a function.

The image of formula_108 under formula_252 denoted by formula_253 is defined as the set formula_254 which necessarily forms a filter base on formula_186 formula_256 is continuous at formula_257 if and only if for every filter base formula_108 on formula_14 formula_260 Cauchy filters. Let formula_261 be a metric space. More generally, given a uniform space formula_14 a filter formula_22 on formula_15 is called a Cauchy filter if for every entourage formula_161 there is an formula_276 with formula_277 In a metric space this agrees with the previous definition. formula_15 is said to be complete if every Cauchy filter converges. Conversely, on a uniform space every convergent filter is a Cauchy filter.

Moreover, every cluster point of a Cauchy filter is a limit point. A compact uniform space is complete: on a compact space each filter has a cluster point, and if the filter is Cauchy, such a cluster point is a limit point. Further, a uniformity is compact if and only if it is complete and totally bounded. Most generally, a Cauchy space is a set equipped with a class of filters declared to be Cauchy. These are required to have the following properties: The Cauchy filters on a uniform space have these properties, so every uniform space (hence every metric space) defines a Cauchy space.
